---
title: "ML Models For Research Project2 Bank Market dataset"
author: "Mr. Pardeshi"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

```{r}



# Load the foreign library (it comes pre-installed with R)
library(foreign)

# Read the ARFF file directly into a Data Frame
Bank_market <- read.arff("E:\\datasets for programming\\dataset\\Bank Market.arff")

# View the structure to confirm it loaded correctly
str(Bank_market)
View(Bank_market)
library(writexl)
write_xlsx(Bank_market,"E:\\datasets for programming\\dataset\\Bank Market.xlsx")
```

Cramer's V is the most common effect size measure for nominal categorical variables, scaling the Chi-Square statistic to a value between 0 and 1.

$V = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{n \cdot \min(r-1, c-1)}}$

Cohen's major contribution was establishing that **the thresholds for Cramer's V are not static; they change based on the degrees of freedom** of the smaller dimension of your contingency table. Let $df^* = \min(r-1, c-1)$, where $r$ is the number of rows and $c$ is the number of columns.

## Identifying Truely Associated categorical variables with Response Variable

```{r}

library(dplyr)
library(rcompanion)

categorical_association <- function(data, response){

  # convert response name
  response <- as.character(response)

  # select categorical variables except response
  cat_vars <- names(data)[sapply(data, function(x) is.factor(x) | is.character(x))]
  cat_vars <- setdiff(cat_vars, response)

  results <- data.frame()

  for (var in cat_vars) {

    tab <- table(data[[var]], data[[response]])

    chi <- chisq.test(tab)

    cramer <- cramerV(tab)

    results <- rbind(results,
                     data.frame(
                       Variable = var,
                       P_value = round(chi$p.value,4),
                       Cramers_V = cramer
                     ))
  }

  results <- results %>% arrange(Cramers_V,descreasing=TRUE)

  return(results)
}

CA=categorical_association(Bank_market,'Class')

# Find the row names where ANY value in that row is > 0.2
var <- rownames(CA)[apply(CA, 1, function(row) any(row > 0.2))] 
```

### Encoding Categorical Feature:

```{r}
# ==============================================================================
# Bank Market Classification: Advanced Target-Based Encoding Pipeline
# ==============================================================================

# 1. LOAD REQUIRED LIBRARIES
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Suppress startup messages for a clean console
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(tidymodels)   # The modern meta-engine for R machine learning
  library(embed)        # Advanced categorical encodings (Target, Bayesian, etc.)
  library(dplyr)        # Data manipulation
  library(xgboost)      # Tree-based model
  library(glmnet)       # Elastic Net model
  library(kknn)         # Distance-based model
  library(kernlab)      # SVM model
})


```

```{r}
# 2. DATA PREPARATION & FEATURE IDENTIFICATION
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Assuming 'Bank_market' is already loaded via the foreign package.
# We will simulate the loading step here for reproducibility.
# Bank_market <- foreign::read.arff("Bank Market.xlsx - Sheet1.arff")

# Ensure Target is a proper factor (tidymodels requires categorical targets for classification)
# Assuming 1 = "Negative" (No Subscribe) and 2 = "Positive" (Subscribe)
Bank_market <- Bank_market %>%
  mutate(
    Class = factor(Class, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Negative", "Positive")),
    # Automatically convert any character columns to factors
    across(where(is.character), as.factor)
)
Bank_market
```

```{r}

# 3. DEFINE PREPROCESSING RECIPES (THE ENCODINGS)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Base recipe: Define formula and normalize numeric predictors (Crucial for SVM/KNN)
base_recipe <- recipe(Class ~ ., data = Bank_market) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) 

# A. Target Mean Encoding (GLM-based Log-Odds)
# Replaces categories with the raw mean of the target variable.
rec_target_mean <- base_recipe %>%
  embed::step_lencode_glm(all_nominal_predictors(), outcome = vars(Class))

# B. Bayesian Mean Encoding (GLMM - Empirical Bayes Shrinkage)
# Uses partial pooling. Rare categories are "shrunk" toward the global mean.
rec_bayesian <- base_recipe %>%
  embed::step_lencode_mixed(all_nominal_predictors(), outcome = vars(Class))

# C. Out-Of-Fold Encoding (LOOE Approximation)
# Note: True LOOE is computationally disastrous for large datasets. 
# tidymodels achieves the exact same mathematical goal (preventing leakage) 
# by calculating the encodings strictly OUTSIDE the current CV assessment fold.
# We use full Bayesian estimation (Stan) here as our 3rd robust technique.
rec_looe_bayes <- base_recipe %>%
  embed::step_lencode_bayes(all_nominal_predictors(), outcome = vars(Class))

# C. Weight of Evidence Encoding (Log-Odds)
rec_woe <- recipe(Class ~ ., data = Bank_market) %>%
  embed::step_woe(all_nominal_predictors(), outcome = vars(Class)) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())



```

### Python Chunk for Catboost

```{r}

# 2. Clean the target variable in R
Bank_market <- Bank_market %>%
  mutate(
    Class = ifelse(Class == 2, 1, 0), # 1 = Subscribe, 0 = No Subscribe
    across(where(is.character), as.factor)
  )

```

```{python python-pipeline}

## 2. The Python Encoding & Modeling Engine
Now, we switch to a Python chunk. The `reticulate` package automatically makes all R objects available in Python through the `r.` object prefix. We will use Python's `category_encoders` library, which contains the highly sought-after `CatBoostEncoder`.

# Import Python Machine Learning Libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import category_encoders as ce
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_validate, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from xgboost import XGBClassifier

# 1. IMPORT DATA FROM R
# Access the R dataframe directly using 'r.'
df = r.Bank_market.copy()

X = df.drop('Class', axis=1)
y = df['Class'].astype(int)

# Identify categorical columns
cat_cols = X.select_dtypes(include=['category', 'object']).columns.tolist()

# 2. DEFINE THE ENCODERS
# Python's category_encoders library supports all of these natively!
encoders = {
    'TargetMean': ce.TargetEncoder(cols=cat_cols, smoothing=10),
    'WoE': ce.WOEEncoder(cols=cat_cols),
    'CatBoost': ce.CatBoostEncoder(cols=cat_cols) # The missing piece from R!
}

# 3. DEFINE THE MODELS
models = {
    'LogReg': LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000),
    'XGBoost': XGBClassifier(use_label_encoder=False, eval_metric='logloss')
}

# 4. CROSS-VALIDATION PIPELINE
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
results_list = []

print("--- Polyglot Cross-Validation Results ---\n")

for enc_name, encoder in encoders.items():
    for mod_name, model in models.items():
        
        # Build a Sklearn Pipeline: Encode -> Scale -> Model
        pipeline = Pipeline([
            ('encoder', encoder),
            ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
            ('classifier', model)
        ])
        
        # Run 5-Fold Cross Validation
        scores = cross_validate(pipeline, X, y, cv=cv, scoring='roc_auc', n_jobs=-1)
        mean_auc = np.mean(scores['test_score'])
        
        results_list.append({
            'Encoder': enc_name,
            'Model': mod_name,
            'ROC_AUC': mean_auc
        })
        
        print(f"[{enc_name} + {mod_name}] ROC-AUC: {mean_auc:.4f}")

# Convert results to a pandas dataframe to easily pass back to R
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results_list)

## 3. Returning to R for Visualization
We can now switch back to an R chunk and access the Python results dataframe using the `py$` object prefix!

library(ggplot2)

# Pull the results_df from Python back into R
r_results <- py$results_df

# Visualize the Polyglot Results
ggplot(r_results, aes(x = Encoder, y = ROC_AUC, fill = Model)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Cross-Language Encoding Benchmark",
    subtitle = "CatBoost Encoding executed in Python, Visualized in R",
    y = "Mean ROC-AUC (5-Fold CV)"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("LogReg" = "#3498db", "XGBoost" = "#e74c3c"))

```

```{r}

# 4. DEFINE MACHINE LEARNING MODELS
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# We instantiate models without extreme tuning to focus on the encoding effects.

mod_logreg <- logistic_reg() %>% 
  set_engine("glm") %>% 
  set_mode("classification")

mod_elastic <- logistic_reg(penalty = 0.01, mixture = 0.5) %>% 
 
   set_engine("glmnet") %>% 
  set_mode("classification")

mod_svm <- svm_rbf(cost = 1) %>% 
  set_engine("kernlab") %>% 
  set_mode("classification")

mod_knn <- nearest_neighbor(neighbors = 5) %>% 
  set_engine("kknn") %>% 
  set_mode("classification")

mod_xgb <- boost_tree(trees = 100) %>% 
  set_engine("xgboost") %>% 
  set_mode("classification")

```

```{r}
# 5. CROSS-VALIDATION SCHEME & WORKFLOW SET
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
set.seed(42)
# 5-Fold Cross Validation, stratified by the target to handle class imbalance
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(Bank_market, v = 5, strata = Class)

# Define the metrics we want to track
cls_metrics <- metric_set(roc_auc, f_meas, accuracy)

# 
# # The "10x Productivity" Move: workflow_set()
# # This creates a Cartesian product of our 3 Encodings x 5 Models = 15 pipelines!
# all_workflows <- workflow_set(
#   preproc = list(
#     TargetMean = rec_target_mean, 
#     Bayesian   = rec_bayesian, 
#     LOOE_Bayes = rec_looe_bayes
#   ),
#   models = list(
#     LogReg     = mod_logreg,
#     ElasticNet = mod_elastic,
#     SVM        = mod_svm,
#     KNN        = mod_knn,
#     XGBoost    = mod_xgb
#   )
# )


```

```{r}

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 10x PRODUCTIVITY FIX: OPTIMIZED WORKFLOW
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# We are removing 'rec_looe_bayes' to bypass the 'rstanarm' dependency and MCMC 
# computation time. We will compare raw Target Mean vs. Empirical Bayes (Mixed).

# Re-define the workflow set with just the two highly efficient encodings
all_workflows_optimized <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(
    TargetMean = rec_target_mean, 
    EmpiricalBayes = rec_bayesian,
    Weight_of_evidence =rec_woe,
    CatBoost   = rec_catboost
    # This will still trigger the singular fit warning, which is statistically fine!
  ),
  models = list(
    LogReg     = mod_logreg,
    ElasticNet = mod_elastic,
    SVM        = mod_svm,
    KNN        = mod_knn,
    XGBoost    = mod_xgb
  )
)

cat("Training 15 optimized pipelines across CV folds...\n")
cat("(Note: You can safely ignore 'boundary (singular) fit' warnings)\n\n")

# Run the workflows
cv_results_optimized <- all_workflows_optimized %>%
  workflow_map(
    "fit_resamples",
    resamples = cv_folds,
    metrics = cls_metrics,
    control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE) 
  )



```

```{r}
# 7. CONSOLIDATED SUMMARY & COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract the results into a clean, readable performance matrix
performance_matrix <- collect_metrics(cv_results_optimized) %>%
  select(wflow_id, .metric, mean) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = .metric, values_from = mean) %>%
  arrange(desc(roc_auc))

cat("\n--- CONSOLIDATED PERFORMANCE MATRIX ---\n")
print(performance_matrix)


# To visualize this locally, you can run: 
# autoplot(cv_results) + theme_minimal() + labs(title = "Encoding vs. Model Performance")
```

### Performance Assessment using Target Based Encoding:

```{r}
# 7. CONSOLIDATED SUMMARY & COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract the results into a clean, readable performance matrix
performance_matrix <- collect_metrics(cv_results_optimized) %>%
  select(wflow_id, .metric, mean) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = .metric, values_from = mean) %>%
  arrange(desc(roc_auc))

cat("\n--- CONSOLIDATED PERFORMANCE MATRIX ---\n")
print(performance_matrix)


# To visualize this locally, you can run: 
# autoplot(cv_results) + theme_minimal() + labs(title = "Encoding vs. Model Performance")
```

### Performance Evaluation Using CatBoost Encoding :

```{r}

all_workflows_optimized_Catboost <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(
    CatBoost   = rec_catboost
    # This will still trigger the singular fit warning, which is statistically fine!
  ),
  models = list(
    LogReg     = mod_logreg,
    ElasticNet = mod_elastic,
    SVM        = mod_svm,
    KNN        = mod_knn,
    XGBoost    = mod_xgb
  )
)


# Run the workflows
cv_results_optimized_Catboost <- all_workflows_optimized_Catboost %>%
  workflow_map(
    "fit_resamples",
    resamples = cv_folds,
    metrics = cls_metrics,
    control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE) 
  )


# Extract the results into a clean, readable performance matrix
performance_matrix_Catboost <- collect_metrics(cv_results_optimized_Catboost) %>%
  select(wflow_id, .metric, mean) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = .metric, values_from = mean) %>%
  arrange(desc(roc_auc))

cat("\n--- CONSOLIDATED PERFORMANCE MATRIX ---\n")
print(performance_matrix)

```

## Python

```{r}

# Load reticulate to enable Python chunks
library(reticulate)
library(dplyr)
library(foreign)

# Specify your Python environment if needed (optional)
# use_condaenv("base") 

# 1. Load the data natively in R
Bank_market <- foreign::read.arff("E:\\datasets for programming\\dataset\\Bank Market.arff")

# 2. Clean the target variable in R
Bank_market <- Bank_market %>%
  mutate(
    Class = ifelse(Class == 2, 1, 0), # 1 = Subscribe, 0 = No Subscribe
    across(where(is.character), as.factor)
  )

```

```{python}

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import category_encoders as ce
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_validate, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore') # Suppress solver convergence warnings for clean output

# 1. IMPORT DATA FROM R
df = r.Bank_market.copy()

# Fix: Force R factors into standard Python strings to avoid category_encoder crashes
cat_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['category', 'object']).columns.tolist()
if 'Class' in cat_cols: cat_cols.remove('Class')
df[cat_cols] = df[cat_cols].astype(str)

X = df.drop('Class', axis=1)
y = df['Class'].astype(int)

# 2. DEFINE THE 5 MODELS
models = {
    'LogReg': LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000),
    'ElasticNet': LogisticRegression(penalty='elasticnet', solver='saga', l1_ratio=0.5, max_iter=1000),
    'KNN': KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5),
    'SVM': SVC(probability=True, max_iter=1000), 
    'XGBoost': XGBClassifier(eval_metric='logloss')
}

# 3. CROSS-VALIDATION PIPELINE (CatBoost Encoding ONLY)
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

# Define multiple scoring metrics
scoring_metrics = ['roc_auc', 'f1', 'accuracy']
results_list = []

print("--- CatBoost Encoding: 5-Fold CV Results ---\n")

for mod_name, model in models.items():
    
    # Build a Sklearn Pipeline: Encode -> Scale -> Model
    pipeline = Pipeline([
        ('encoder', ce.CatBoostEncoder(cols=cat_cols)),
        ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
        ('classifier', model)
    ])
    
    # Run 5-Fold Cross Validation for multiple metrics
    scores = cross_validate(pipeline, X, y, cv=cv, scoring=scoring_metrics, n_jobs=-1)
    
    mean_auc = np.mean(scores['test_roc_auc'])
    mean_f1  = np.mean(scores['test_f1'])
    mean_acc = np.mean(scores['test_accuracy'])
    
    results_list.append({
        'Encoder': 'CatBoost',
        'Model': mod_name,
        'ROC_AUC': mean_auc,
        'F1_Score': mean_f1,
        'Accuracy': mean_acc
    })
    
    print(f"[{mod_name}] AUC: {mean_auc:.4f} | F1: {mean_f1:.4f} | Acc: {mean_acc:.4f}")

# Convert results to a pandas dataframe to easily pass back to R
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results_list)

results_df
```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)


# Pull the results_df from Python back into R
r_results <- py$results_df

# 10x Data Scientist Move: Reshape the data from Wide to Long format
r_results_long <- r_results %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(ROC_AUC, F1_Score, Accuracy),
    names_to = "Metric",
    values_to = "Score"
  )

# Visualize the comprehensive performance matrix
ggplot(r_results_long, aes(x = reorder(Model, Score), y = Score, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() + 
  facet_wrap(~Metric, scales = "free_x") + # Creates 3 separate panels!
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14), # Bold the panel headers
    panel.spacing = unit(2, "lines") # Add breathing room between plots
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Comprehensive Model Performance using CatBoost Encoding",
    subtitle = "Comparing ROC-AUC, F1-Score, and Accuracy via 5-Fold CV",
    x = "Machine Learning Model",
    y = "Evaluation Score"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(guide = "none") + 
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", Score)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1.15)) # Give text room to breathe

```

## Recall For Comparison Purpose

```{r}

# ==============================================================================
# Bank Market Classification: Target Encoding Visualization Dashboard
# ==============================================================================

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(tidymodels)   
  library(embed)        
  library(dplyr)
  library(tidyr)        # For data reshaping (separate and pivot)
  library(ggplot2)      # For 10x Data Visualization
  library(xgboost)      
  library(glmnet)       
  library(kknn)         
  library(kernlab)      
})

# 1. LOAD & PREP DATA
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bank_market <- foreign::read.arff("E:\\datasets for programming\\dataset\\Bank Market.arff") %>%
  mutate(
    Class = factor(Class, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("Negative", "Positive")),
    across(where(is.character), as.factor)
  )

# 2. DEFINE RECIPES (Target Mean, Bayesian, WoE)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
rec_target_mean <- recipe(Class ~ ., data = Bank_market) %>%
  embed::step_lencode_glm(all_nominal_predictors(), outcome = vars(Class)) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) 

rec_bayesian <- recipe(Class ~ ., data = Bank_market) %>%
  embed::step_lencode_mixed(all_nominal_predictors(), outcome = vars(Class)) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

rec_woe <- recipe(Class ~ ., data = Bank_market) %>%
  embed::step_woe(all_nominal_predictors(), outcome = vars(Class)) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

# 3. DEFINE MODELS & WORKFLOW
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
mod_logreg <- logistic_reg() %>% set_engine("glm") %>% set_mode("classification")
mod_elastic <- logistic_reg(penalty = 0.01, mixture = 0.5) %>% set_engine("glmnet") %>% set_mode("classification")
mod_svm <- svm_rbf(cost = 1) %>% set_engine("kernlab") %>% set_mode("classification")
mod_knn <- nearest_neighbor(neighbors = 5) %>% set_engine("kknn") %>% set_mode("classification")
mod_xgb <- boost_tree(trees = 100) %>% set_engine("xgboost") %>% set_mode("classification")

all_workflows <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(TargetMean = rec_target_mean, Bayesian = rec_bayesian, WoE = rec_woe),
  models = list(LogReg = mod_logreg, ElasticNet = mod_elastic, SVM = mod_svm, KNN = mod_knn, XGBoost = mod_xgb)
)

# 4. TRAIN (5-Fold CV)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
set.seed(42)
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(Bank_market, v = 5, strata = Class)
cls_metrics <- metric_set(roc_auc, f_meas, accuracy)

cat("Training 15 pipelines... please wait.\n")
cv_results <- all_workflows %>%
  workflow_map("fit_resamples", resamples = cv_folds, metrics = cls_metrics)

# 5. DATA WRANGLING FOR VISUALIZATION (The 10x Trick)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
plot_data <- collect_metrics(cv_results) %>%
  filter(.metric %in% c("roc_auc", "f_meas", "accuracy")) %>%
  # Magic Step: Split 'WoE_XGBoost' into Encoder='WoE' and Model='XGBoost'
  separate(wflow_id, into = c("Encoder", "Model"), sep = "_", extra = "merge") %>%
  mutate(
    # Clean up metric names for the plot titles
    Metric = case_when(
      .metric == "roc_auc" ~ "ROC_AUC",
      .metric == "f_meas" ~ "F1_Score",
      .metric == "accuracy" ~ "Accuracy"
    )
  )

# 6. FACETED GGPLOT DASHBOARD
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Create a grouped bar chart using position_dodge()
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = reorder(Model, mean), y = mean, fill = Encoder)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), color = "black", alpha = 0.85, width = 1.0) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~Metric, scales = "free_x") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    panel.spacing = unit(2, "lines"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Encoder vs. Algorithm Performance Dashboard",
    subtitle = "Comparing Target Mean, Bayesian, and WoE across 5 Model Families",
    x = "Machine Learning Algorithm",
    y = "Evaluation Score",
    fill = "Encoding Strategy:"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "mako") + # 'mako' gives a highly professional blue/purple palette
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", mean)), 
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), 
    hjust = -0.15, 
    size = 3.2
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1.15)) # Extra space for the text labels

```

```{r}
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| fig-cap: 
#|   - "Before Transformation"
#|   - "After Transformation"

x=runif(1000,2,10)
hist(x)
hist(log(x))
```

```{r}

#| label: calculate-categorical-associations
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Generalized Cramer's V Function"

get_significant_associations <- function(data) {
  
  # 1. Isolate only categorical or character columns
  cat_cols <- names(data)[sapply(data, function(x) is.factor(x) || is.character(x))]
  
  if(length(cat_cols) < 2) {
    stop("The dataset must contain at least two categorical variables.")
  }
  
  results <- data.frame()
  
  # 2. Iterate through all unique pairs of categorical variables
  for(i in 1:(length(cat_cols)-1)) {
    for(j in (i+1):length(cat_cols)) {
      var1 <- cat_cols[i]
      var2 <- cat_cols[j]
      
      # Create a contingency table (ignoring missing values)
      tbl <- table(data[[var1]], data[[var2]])
      
      # Ensure the table has at least 2x2 dimensions to calculate Chi-Square
      if(nrow(tbl) > 1 && ncol(tbl) > 1) {
        
        # Calculate Chi-Square statistic
        chi2 <- suppressWarnings(chisq.test(tbl, correct = FALSE)$statistic)
        n <- sum(tbl)
        
        # Calculate df* (minimum dimension minus 1)
        df_star <- min(nrow(tbl) - 1, ncol(tbl) - 1)
        
        # Calculate Cramer's V
        v <- sqrt(chi2 / (n * df_star))
        
        # 3. Dynamically apply Cohen's Thresholds based on df*
        if(df_star == 1) {
          med_thresh <- 0.30; lrg_thresh <- 0.50
        } else if (df_star == 2) {
          med_thresh <- 0.21; lrg_thresh <- 0.35
        } else if (df_star == 3) {
          med_thresh <- 0.17; lrg_thresh <- 0.29
        } else {
          med_thresh <- 0.15; lrg_thresh <- 0.25 # For df* >= 4
        }
        
        # Classify the effect size
        strength <- ifelse(v >= lrg_thresh, "Large",
                           ifelse(v >= med_thresh, "Medium", "Weak"))
        
        # Append to results
        results <- rbind(results, data.frame(
          Variable_1 = var1,
          Variable_2 = var2,
          df_star = df_star,
          Cramers_V = round(v, 4),
          Effect_Size = strength
        ))
      }
    }
  }
  
  # 4. Filter for significance and sort descending
  significant_results <- results[results$Effect_Size %in% c("Medium", "Large"), ]
  significant_results <- significant_results[order(-significant_results$Cramers_V), ]
  row.names(significant_results) <- NULL
  
  return(significant_results)
}

# --- Example Usage ---
# df <- read.csv("your_data.csv")
strong_features <- get_significant_associations(Bank_market)
print(strong_features)
```

```{}
```

```{r}

#| label: fig-categorical-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Interactive Heatmap of Significant Categorical Associations"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 8
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)

# Assuming 'strong_features' is the data frame generated from the previous function

# 1. Create a mirrored dataset so the heatmap matrix is symmetrical
heatmap_data <- rbind(
  strong_features[, c("Variable_1", "Variable_2", "Cramers_V", "Effect_Size")],
  data.frame(Variable_1 = strong_features$Variable_2,
             Variable_2 = strong_features$Variable_1,
             Cramers_V = strong_features$Cramers_V,
             Effect_Size = strong_features$Effect_Size)
)

# 2. Build the static ggplot with a custom HTML tooltip aesthetic
p <- ggplot(heatmap_data, aes(x = Variable_1, y = Variable_2, fill = Cramers_V,
                              text = paste("<b>Feature 1:</b>", Variable_1,
                                           "<br><b>Feature 2:</b>", Variable_2,
                                           "<br><b>Cramer's V:</b>", Cramers_V,
                                           "<br><b>Effect Size:</b>", Effect_Size))) +
  # Draw the tiles
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  # Use a clean, colorblind-friendly gradient (white -> blue -> dark orange)
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#f7f7f7", mid = "#67a9cf", high = "#ef8a62", 
                       midpoint = 0.25, limit = c(0, 1), space = "Lab", 
                       name="Cramer's V") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, size = 12, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank()
  ) +
  # Keep the tiles perfectly square
  coord_fixed()

# 3. Convert to a highly interactive HTML widget
# The 'tooltip = "text"' tells plotly to use our custom formatted string
ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    # Add extra margins so long variable names are completely visible
    margin = list(b = 120, l = 120),
    # Tidy up the hover interaction
    hoverlabel = list(bgcolor = "white", font = list(size = 14))
  )
```
